content.assessment: The Finnish lens offers little direct evidence on fiscal-conditionality mechanisms, as Finland's reform was structural and provision-based rather than funding-incentive-based. This proposal does not engage tracking age or comprehensive provision at all, instead operating through a fiscal lever entirely outside the scope of the Finnish comprehensive-school model. To the extent the Finnish case is informative, it suggests that formal structural changes (unification, delayed tracking) were necessary to produce measured equity gains, and that even structural changes required decades and complementary reforms to take effect (F-live-finnish_reform-05); a funding-incentive mechanism operating alongside an unchanged tracking structure has no analog in the Finnish reform pathway and its efficacy cannot be assessed through this lens.
opportunities: Could theoretically complement structural reform if adopted alongside tracking-age changes, though this is speculative and unsupported by the lens evidence
strengths: Does not conflict with any Finnish-lens criterion, since it operates on a different mechanism (fiscal incentives) than structural tracking reform
threats: Finland's history shows that even robust structural reforms with two decades of implementation faced later erosion via informal selection mechanisms (F-live-finnish_reform-06); a fiscal-only lever, being more indirect than structural reform, may be even more susceptible to circumvention by maintainers adjusting non-SES criteria
weaknesses: Does not address tracking age or comprehensive provision, the two central variables the Finnish lens evaluates
No Finnish or comparable evidence exists in the supplied findings regarding fiscal-conditionality mechanisms as a lever for reducing tracking-driven segregation
